title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Seagrasses are the only flowering plants that grow in marine environments, (0) forming vast underwater meadows that are essential for ocean health. (9) Although they are frequently confused with seaweed, they are actually more closely related to terrestrial grasses and lilies. (10) Such is the capacity of seagrass to capture carbon that it can store it up to thirty-five times faster than a rainforest. (11) Provided that the water remains clear and unpolluted, these meadows can provide a vital nursery for thousands of fish and invertebrate species. (12) Not only do they support biodiversity, but they also protect coastlines by reducing the energy of waves and preventing erosion. (13) Regardless of their ecological value, seagrass habitats are disappearing at an alarming rate due to human activity and coastal development. (14) Not until recently have scientists fully mapped the extent of these meadows and their contribution to fighting climate change. (15) In spite of the damage already sustained, restoration efforts in places like the UK are showing promising results for the future. (16) Whether we can successfully protect these hidden ecosystems will depend on global cooperation and awareness. These green fields of the sea are a silent but powerful ally in our environmental efforts.

example.text: Seagrasses are the only flowering plants that grow in marine environments, (0) forming vast underwater meadows.
example.explanation: Present participle used to describe the result of an action or a secondary characteristic.

questions.explanation: Conjunction introducing a contrast or concession.
Determiner used for emphasis at the start of the clause.
Part of the 'Not only... but also' correlative conjunction.
Part of the fixed phrase 'Regardless of'.
Part of the 'Not until' structure requiring inversion ('have scientists... mapped').
Part of the prepositional linker 'In spite of'.
Conjunction introducing two possibilities or a doubt.
